items.abstract: <jats:title>Abstract</jats:title><jats:p> <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="#b2">Cunha and Caldieraro (2009)</jats:ext-link> investigated whether sunk‐cost effects, which are well documented in hypothetical situations involving monetary investments, also occur in choice situations with purely behavioral investments. Their results suggest that decision makers indeed fall prey to behavioral sunk‐cost effects under certain circumstances. I have been unable to replicate their pattern of results in three separate investigations. In these studies, I attempted to recover the effect using two other behavioral effort manipulations in addition to the manipulation used by Cunha and Caldieraro. This failure to replicate the pattern of results calls into question the robustness of the initial findings.</jats:p>
title: Three Attempts to Replicate the Behavioral Sunk‐Cost Effect: A Note on Cunha and Caldieraro (2009)

items.abstract: <jats:p>The present work includes three attempts to replicate the moral licensing effect by Sachdeva, Iliev, and Medin (2009) . The original authors found that writing about positive traits led to lower donations to charity and decreased cooperative behavior. The first two replication attempts (student samples, 95% power based on the initial findings, N<jats:sub>Study1</jats:sub>= 105, N<jats:sub>Study2</jats:sub>= 150), did not confirm the original results. The third replication attempt (MTurk sample, 95% power based on a meta-analysis on self-licensing, N = 940) also did not confirm the moral licensing effect. We conclude that (1) there is as of yet no strong support for the moral self-regulation framework proposed in Sachdeva et al. (2009) (2) the manipulation used is unlikely to induce moral licensing, and (3) studies on moral licensing should use a neutral control condition.</jats:p>
title: Three Attempts to Replicate the Moral Licensing Effect

items.abstract: <jats:p> Three studies replicated a classroom experiment on single-exposure musical conditioning of consumer choice ( Gorn, 1982 ), testing whether simultaneous exposure to liked (vs. disliked) music and a pen image induced preferences for the shown (vs. a different) pen. Experiments 1 and 2 employed the original music, Experiment 3 used contemporary music. Experiments 2 and 3 employed hypothesis-blind experimenters. All studies incorporated post-experimental inquiries exploring demand artifacts. Experiments 1 and 2 (original music; N = 158, N = 190) showed no evidence for musical conditioning, and were qualified (conclusive) replication failures. Experiment 3 (contemporary music; N = 91) reproduced original effects, but with significantly smaller effect size. Moreover, it had limited power and showed extreme scores in one experimental group. Aggregated, the three studies produced a null effect. Exploration of demand artifacts suggests they are unlikely to have produced the original results. </jats:p>

items.abstract: <jats:p>The paper examines the experience of financial integration in East Asia in the 1990s, with a particular focus on the unsuccessful attempt to create an Asian financial fund. The study covers key initiatives for regional financial integration, including ASEAN programs, and analyzes the reasons for &nbsp;the failure of the Japanese initiative to establish the Asian Monetary Fund. The first part of the paper examines the main stages of financial integration in the region, including the adoption of the ASEAN Vision for 2020, the development of a Roadmap for monetary and financial integration, and the creation of the ASEAN Financial Integration Framework (AFIF). Special attention is paid to the analysis of the effectiveness of these initiatives and their impact on the development of regional cooperation. The second part of the paper is devoted to the analysis of the reasons for the failure of the Japanese initiative to create the Asian Monetary Fund. Both internal factors (differences in the national interests of the countries of the region) and external opposition from the United States, which played a decisive role in the failure of the project, are considered. The study shows that despite significant efforts towards regional financial integration, the success of these initiatives is limited by a number of factors, including political differences, differences in the level of development of national financial systems, and external influence.</jats:p>
title: Attempts at financial integration in East Asia (1990s). Unsuccessful Japanese initiative to create an Asian financial fund

title: A biomechanical comparison of successful and unsuccessful power clean attempts

items.abstract: <jats:p>The success factor of the snatch has not been identified. Determining the success factors of the snatch among elite weightlifters might help to attain a successful snatch. This study aimed at clarifying the factors that lead to a successful snatch based on barbell trajectory among elite male weightlifters. Data were collected at the 2017 World and Junior World Weightlifting Championships. We digitized the barbell trajectory of the successful and unsuccessful snatch attempts of 61 lifters—an unsuccessful lift would be as a result of a frontward barbell drop—and calculated the kinematic and kinetic parameters of the barbell. No significant difference was found in the barbell maximum height (Dy1) between the successful and unsuccessful lifts. The amount of backward displacement of the barbell in the second pull phase to the catch position (DxL) of the successful lift was significantly larger than that of the unsuccessful lift (successful: 0.11 ± 0.05 m; unsuccessful: 0.10 ± 0.06 m; p < 0.01; d = 0.278). The barbell drop distance in the catch phase (Dy3) of the successful lift was significantly smaller than that in the unsuccessful lift (successful: 0.17 ± 0.04 m; unsuccessful: 0.18 ± 0.04 m; p < 0.001, d = 0.361). These results suggest that DxL and Dy3 are factors leading to a successful snatch lift, but not Dy1. The relative position in the sagittal axis of the barbell and the lifter in the catch position, and catching the barbell when its momentum was low, are important in order to achieve a successful snatch.</jats:p>
